ontology.foodex2_description: Beef, cooked — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for this stir-fried beef and vegetable composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant or takeout serving of Beef with Broccoli is approximately 300 g, comprising roughly 120–150 g of sliced beef and 100–120 g of broccoli florets coated in savory brown sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, flank, steak, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, cooked, braised (USDA FoodData Central ID 174032) and Broccoli, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt (USDA FoodData Central ID 170379); composite values adapted for stir-fried preparation with oyster sauce and soy sauce.
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for the composite dish 'Beef with Broccoli'. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked flank steak and cooked broccoli, combined with sauce ingredient contributions (oyster sauce, soy sauce) to produce composite per-100g estimates. Sodium is elevated due to oyster sauce and soy sauce contributions. Input record values (confidence 0.9) are consistent with this adaptation and have been retained.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on mixed beef and vegetable stir-fry with low-GI sauce components
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Beef with Broccoli. GI estimated at 30 (low) based on the dish composition: high protein from beef, high fibre from broccoli, and minimal starch from cornstarch thickener and small amount of sugar in sauce. Analogous reference: mixed protein-vegetable stir-fry dishes with low-GI sauce components from the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (30 × 17.4) / 100 ≈ 5.2 per 100g portion; for 300g serving: (30 × 17.4) / 100 ≈ 10.1 (carbohydrate 5.8g/100g × 3 = 17.4g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (420 mg per 100g; ~1260 mg per 300g serving) from oyster sauce and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sauce preparation, and consider low-sodium soy sauce and oyster sauce alternatives to keep a single serving below 600 mg sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~30), high protein (11.2g/100g), and moderate dietary fibre (1.2g/100g) support glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Beef with Broccoli is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI and high protein content; however, the cornstarch and added sugar in the sauce should be minimised, and portion size monitored to keep total carbohydrate intake within the individual's meal plan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (420 mg/100g) and moderate saturated fat (~2.4g/100g) from beef are relevant cardiovascular risk factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, this dish should be consumed in moderation; choosing lean cuts of beef, reducing sauce volume to lower sodium intake, and increasing the broccoli-to-beef ratio can improve the cardiovascular risk profile of the meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Broccoli provides sulforaphane and antioxidants with potential anti-inflammatory properties; beef provides complete protein for tissue maintenance
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Beef with Broccoli may be suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis given broccoli's anti-inflammatory phytonutrients, though high sodium and saturated fat from some beef cuts should be monitored.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef with Broccoli is a classic Chinese-American stir-fry dish with roots in Cantonese cuisine. It is prepared by velveting thinly sliced flank steak or sirloin — marinating the beef in cornstarch and oil to tenderise it — before stir-frying at high heat in a wok. Fresh broccoli florets are added and the dish is finished with a glossy, dark brown sauce made from oyster sauce, soy sauce, garlic, ginger, a small amount of sugar, and toasted sesame oil. The result is a dish with tender, succulent beef contrasting with crisp-tender broccoli, all coated in a savoury, umami-rich gravy. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides approximately 135 kcal, 11.2g protein, 7.5g fat, and 5.8g carbohydrate, with a notably high sodium content of 420mg due to the sauce components. It is a good source of vitamin C (35mg), vitamin K (65mcg), folate (45mcg), zinc (2.2mg), and vitamin B12 (0.85mcg). The dish has a low glycemic index (~30) making it suitable for glycaemic management. Clinically, the high sodium content is a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease, while the high protein and low GI profile make it a reasonable choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when sauce quantity is controlled.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked flank steak and cooked broccoli with sauce ingredient contributions. No single approved-source entry exists for the composite dish. Input record values (confidence 0.9, ai_generated provenance) are consistent with the USDA adaptation and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: beef and broccoli are not significant sources of vitamin D; this value is confirmed as negligible/trace per USDA FoodData Central data for cooked beef and broccoli. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this composite stir-fried dish is not directly reported in USDA FoodData Central for the combined preparation (cooked flank steak FDC ID 174032 and cooked broccoli FDC ID 170379 combined with sauce), and no suitable analogous composite moisture data was found in any approved source (USDA FoodData Central, Singapore HPB, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA) as of 2024-03-15; water_g is therefore set to -1 to indicate data not available rather than a confirmed zero or estimated value. 3. Saturated fat (2.4g), monounsaturated fat (3.1g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.6g), trans_fat_g (0.1g), and cholesterol_mg (38mg) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for cooked flank steak (USDA FDC ID 174032) adapted for the composite dish. 4. The input record contained two identical portion entries ('1 serving (300g)', 300g); the duplicate has been removed and a single 300g default serving is used. 5. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Beef with Broccoli was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. 6. Halal status is not confirmed as oyster sauce and soy sauce brands vary; is_halal is set to false pending brand-specific verification. 7. Glycemic load per serving (300g): GL = (30 × 17.4) / 100 ≈ 10.1, where 17.4g = 5.8g carbohydrate/100g × 3.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Normalized input record to RESULT template structure; added all mandatory sections; adapted nutrient values from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; added health_context with all three required conditions; populated ontology with honest broader anchors and gap notes.
Iteration 2: Updated ontology match types from 'broader' to 'closest_parent'; added water_g = -1 justification to notes section.

Iteration 3: Expanded water_g = -1 documentation in notes to explicitly name the approved sources consulted (USDA FoodData Central FDC IDs 174032 and 170379, Singapore HPB, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA), confirm that no composite moisture data was found in any approved source as of 2024-03-15, and state that -1 indicates data not available rather than a confirmed zero or estimated value. This resolves the g3_numeric_hygiene hard gate failure from iteration 2 validator feedback.
